name: Python
tagline: Readable, versatile, batteries included.
tips: Use list comprehensions instead of loops: `[x*2 for x in nums if x > 0]`.
Swap variables without a temp: `a, b = b, a`.
Use `enumerate()` instead of manual counters in loops.
f-strings support debugging: `print(f'{value=}')` prints name and value.
Use `collections.Counter` for instant frequency counts.
Prefer `pathlib.Path` over `os.path` for file operations.
Virtual environments always: `python -m venv .venv`.
snippet:
from collections import Counter
words = 'the quick the lazy the'.split()
print(Counter(words).most_common(1))  # [('the', 3)]

name: JavaScript
tagline: The language of the web — everywhere, always.
tips: Always use `===` over `==` to avoid type-coercion surprises.
Destructure objects for cleaner code: `const { name, age } = user;`.
Optional chaining prevents crashes: `user?.profile?.avatar`.
Nullish coalescing for defaults: `value ?? 'fallback'` (unlike `||`, keeps 0 and '').
Use `Promise.all()` to run async operations in parallel.
`structuredClone(obj)` deep-copies without JSON hacks.
Debounce expensive event handlers (scroll, resize, input).

name: TypeScript
tagline: JavaScript that scales — with types.
tips: Enable `strict: true` in tsconfig — it catches whole bug classes.
Prefer `unknown` over `any`; it forces you to narrow types.
Use utility types: `Partial<T>`, `Pick<T, K>`, `Omit<T, K>`, `Record<K, V>`.
Discriminated unions make impossible states impossible.
`as const` freezes literals into precise types.
Let inference work — don't annotate what TS already knows.

name: Java
tagline: Write once, run anywhere. Enterprise workhorse.
tips: Use Streams for collection pipelines: `list.stream().filter(...).map(...).toList()`.
Prefer `Optional<T>` over returning null.
Records (Java 16+) kill boilerplate: `record Point(int x, int y) {}`.
Use `var` for obvious local types (Java 10+).
StringBuilder for loops that build strings.
Try-with-resources auto-closes files and connections.

name: C++
tagline: Maximum performance, maximum control.
tips: Use smart pointers (`unique_ptr`, `shared_ptr`) — avoid raw `new`/`delete`.
RAII everything: resources tied to object lifetime never leak.
Pass large objects by `const&`, return by value (move semantics handle it).
Prefer `std::vector` over raw arrays; `std::array` for fixed size.
Use `auto` for iterator types and lambdas.
Range-based for loops: `for (const auto& item : items)`.
snippet:
auto ptr = std::make_unique<Widget>();
std::ranges::sort(items, {}, &Item::price); // C++20 ranges

name: Go
tagline: Simple, fast, built for cloud concurrency.
tips: Goroutines are cheap — but always know how they exit.
Use channels to communicate; don't communicate by sharing memory.
`defer` cleanup right after acquiring a resource.
Handle every error explicitly — it's a feature, not noise.
Table-driven tests are idiomatic and scale beautifully.
`go fmt` and `go vet` before every commit.

name: Rust
tagline: Memory safety without garbage collection.
tips: Fight the borrow checker less: clone early while learning, optimize later.
Use `match` exhaustively — the compiler ensures you handle every case.
`?` operator propagates errors cleanly: `let file = File::open(path)?;`.
Iterators are zero-cost: prefer `.iter().map().filter()` chains over loops.
`cargo clippy` teaches you idiomatic Rust as you code.
Use `Option`/`Result` — there is no null, and that's the point.

name: C#
tagline: Modern, elegant, the heart of .NET.
tips: LINQ makes collection queries readable: `people.Where(p => p.Age > 18).Select(p => p.Name)`.
Use `async`/`await` all the way down — never `.Result` or `.Wait()`.
Records for immutable data: `record Person(string Name, int Age);`.
Pattern matching is powerful: `if (obj is Person { Age: > 18 } p)`.
Null-conditional operators: `user?.Address?.City ?? "Unknown"`.
String interpolation: `$"Hello {name}, you are {age}"`.
snippet:
var topScores = players
    .OrderByDescending(p => p.Score)
    .Take(10)
    .Select(p => $"{p.Name}: {p.Score}");

name: PHP
tagline: Powers 75% of the web — and modern PHP is good.
tips: Use Composer for everything — never copy-paste libraries.
Enable strict types: `declare(strict_types=1);` at the top of files.
Null coalescing: `$name = $_GET['name'] ?? 'guest';`.
Arrow functions (7.4+): `fn($x) => $x * 2`.
Match expressions (8.0+) beat switch: no fallthrough, returns values.
Always use prepared statements — never concatenate SQL.

name: Swift
tagline: Fast, safe, expressive — Apple's modern language.
tips: Use `guard let` for early exits — keeps the happy path unindented.
Prefer `let` over `var`; immutability by default.
Optionals are your friend: `if let`, `??`, and optional chaining.
Value types (structs) by default; classes only when you need reference semantics.
`enum` with associated values models state elegantly.
Use trailing closure syntax for readability.
snippet:
guard let user = fetchUser(id) else { return }
let names = users.compactMap { $0.nickname }

name: Kotlin
tagline: Concise, safe, 100% Java interop — Android's darling.
tips: Data classes give you equals/hashCode/toString for free.
Null safety built-in: `String?` vs `String`, use `?.` and `?:`.
Scope functions: `let`, `apply`, `also`, `run`, `with` — learn when each fits.
Extension functions add behavior without inheritance.
Coroutines make async code look synchronous.
`when` expressions are exhaustive and expressive.
snippet:
data class User(val name: String, val email: String?)
val domain = user.email?.substringAfter("@") ?: "unknown"

name: SQL
tagline: The language of data — 50 years strong.
tips: Index columns you filter and join on — but not everything.
`EXPLAIN ANALYZE` reveals why queries are slow.
Use CTEs (`WITH`) to make complex queries readable.
Window functions beat self-joins: `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ...)`.
`COALESCE()` handles nulls in results gracefully.
Never `SELECT *` in production code.

name: Ruby
tagline: Optimized for developer happiness.
tips: Everything is an object — even integers have methods: `5.times { }`.
Use symbols (`:name`) for identifiers, strings for data.
Safe navigation: `user&.profile&.avatar`.
Blocks, procs, and lambdas are Ruby's superpower — master them.
`Enumerable` methods (`map`, `select`, `reduce`, `each_slice`) replace most loops.
String interpolation over concatenation: `"Hello #{name}"`.

name: C (Embedded)
tagline: The bedrock of firmware and bare-metal control.
tips: Mark memory-mapped registers `volatile` so the compiler never caches them.
Use fixed-width types from `<stdint.h>`: `uint8_t`, `int32_t` — never rely on `int` size.
Set/clear/toggle bits with masks: `PORTB |= (1 << PIN);` and `PORTB &= ~(1 << PIN);`.
Avoid dynamic `malloc()` on tiny MCUs — prefer static/stack allocation to dodge fragmentation.
Keep interrupt service routines (ISRs) short; just set a flag and handle work in the main loop.
Put read-only tables in flash with `const` to save precious RAM.

name: Assembly
tagline: Machine-level control, one instruction at a time.
tips: Learn your target’s register set and calling convention before anything else.
ARM (Thumb) dominates MCUs; x86-64 dominates PCs — the mental model differs a lot.
Use assembly only for hot paths or ISRs; write the rest in C and inspect the disassembly.
Inline assembly in C via `asm volatile(...)` lets you touch instructions C can’t express.
Keep the chip’s instruction-set datasheet open — opcodes and cycle counts live there.
Compiler Explorer (godbolt) shows exactly what your C compiles down to.
snippet:
@ ARM: add two registers
    ADD r0, r1, r2   @ r0 = r1 + r2
    BX  lr           @ return

name: Verilog
tagline: Describe digital hardware, synthesize to silicon.
tips: Use non-blocking `<=` for sequential (clocked) logic, blocking `=` for combinational.
Clocked logic lives in `always @(posedge clk)`; keep resets consistent (sync vs async).
Stick to the synthesizable subset — not every legal Verilog maps to gates.
Fully specify combinational `always` blocks (or use `always @*`) to avoid accidental latches.
`wire` for continuous assignments, `reg` for values assigned inside `always` blocks.
Simulate with a testbench before you ever touch the FPGA.
snippet:
module dff(input clk, input d, output reg q);
  always @(posedge clk)
    q <= d;   // D flip-flop
endmodule

name: VHDL
tagline: Strongly-typed HDL for mission-critical hardware.
tips: It’s strongly typed: use `std_logic` / `std_logic_vector` and convert explicitly.
Every design is an `entity` (ports) plus one or more `architecture` bodies.
List every read signal in a process’s sensitivity list, or use `process(all)` (VHDL-2008).
Signals update at the next delta cycle; variables update immediately — know the difference.
Use `ieee.numeric_std` (`signed`/`unsigned`) for arithmetic, not the deprecated std_logic_arith.
Its verbosity is a feature — it catches width and type mismatches at compile time.

name: SystemVerilog
tagline: Verilog supercharged for verification.
tips: Build reusable testbenches with classes and OOP — the backbone of the UVM methodology.
Catch protocol bugs with SystemVerilog Assertions (SVA): `assert property(@(posedge clk) req |-> ##2 ack);`
Drive corner cases using constrained-random: `rand` variables plus `constraint` blocks.
Measure completeness with functional coverage (`covergroup` / `coverpoint`).
Use `interface` and `clocking` blocks to cleanly connect testbench to DUT and avoid races.
Keep synthesizable RTL separate from verification-only constructs.

name: MATLAB
tagline: Math, modeling, and DSP for engineers.
tips: Vectorize: operate on whole arrays instead of writing element-by-element loops.
Preallocate arrays (`z = zeros(1,N);`) before filling them to avoid slow reallocation.
Use `fft()` for spectral analysis and `filter()`/`conv()` for DSP pipelines.
Model whole communication/control systems visually in Simulink before building hardware.
Lean on toolboxes — Signal Processing, Communications, Control System — instead of reinventing.
End statements with `;` to suppress console echo in long scripts.

name: Tcl
tagline: The scripting glue of EDA tool flows.
tips: Everything is a string — quoting and `{braces}` (defer eval) vs `"quotes"` (substitute) matter.
Vivado, Synopsys Design Compiler, and Cadence tools all drive their consoles with Tcl.
Define reusable steps with `proc name {args} { ... }` and `source` shared scripts.
Set variables with `set x 5` and read them with `$x`; commands go in `[brackets]`.
Timing constraints (`.sdc`) are literally Tcl — `create_clock`, `set_input_delay`, etc.
Use `foreach` and `lindex`/`llength` for list-driven automation over pins and nets.

name: Perl
tagline: Text-munging workhorse in chip design.
tips: Its killer feature is regex: `if ($line =~ /ERROR:\s+(\d+)/) { ... }`.
Always start scripts with `use strict; use warnings;` to catch bugs early.
`$_` is the implicit default variable for loops and matches — concise but read carefully.
Hashes give O(1) lookups: `my %seen; $seen{$key}++;`.
One-liners are gold for log crunching: `perl -ne 'print if /FAIL/' run.log`.
CPAN has a module for almost everything — check before writing your own.
